volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Ghost (Section 1.3.258b - Section 1.4.333c)

sections.label: Section 1.3.258b
sections.original: Quid cerussa opust nám ?
sections.translation: Why , what need of ceruse have you ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.258c
sections.original: Qui malas oblinam .
sections.translation: To paint my cheeks with it .

sections.label: Section 1.3.259
sections.original: Vna operá ebur atramento candefacere postules .
sections.translation: On the same principle , you would want to be making ivory white with ink .

sections.label: Section 1.3.260
sections.original: Lepide dictum de atramento atque ebore . euge , plaudo Scaphae .
sections.translation: Cleverly said that , about the ink and the ivory ! Bravo ! I applaud you , Scapha .

sections.label: Section 1.3.261
sections.original: Tum tu igitur cedo purpurissum .
sections.translation: Well then , do you give me the rouge .

sections.label: Section 1.3.261b
sections.original: Non do . scita es tu quidem .
sections.translation: I shan ’ t give it . You really are a clever one . Do you wish to patch up a most clever piece with new daubing ? It ’ s not right that any paint should touch that person , neither ceruse , nor quince-ointment , nor any other wash .

sections.label: Section 1.3.265
sections.original: Cape igitur speculum .
sections.translation: Take the mirror , then . ( Hands her the glass .)

sections.label: Section 1.3.265b
sections.original: Ei mihi misero , savium speculo dedit .
sections.translation: Ah wretched me !— she gave the glass a kiss . I could much wish for a stone , with which to break the head of that glass .

sections.label: Section 1.3.267
sections.original: Linteum cape atque exterge tibi manus .
sections.translation: Take the towel and wipe your hands .

sections.label: Section 1.3.267b
sections.original: Quid ita , obsecro ?
sections.translation: Why so , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.268
sections.original: Vt speculum tenuisti , metuo né olant argentum manus :
sections.translation: As you ’ ve been holding the mirror , I ’ m afraid that your hands may smell of silver ; lest Philolaches should suspect you ’ ve been receiving silver somewhere .

sections.label: Section 1.3.270
sections.original: Non videor vidisse lenam callidiorem ullam alteras .
sections.translation: I don ’ t think that I ever did see any one procuress more cunning . How cleverly and artfully did it occur to the jade ’ s imagination about the mirror !

sections.label: Section 1.3.272
sections.original: Etiamne unguentis unguendam censes ?
sections.translation: Do you think I ought to be perfumed with unguents as well ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.272b
sections.original: Minime feceris .
sections.translation: By no means do so .

sections.label: Section 1.3.273
sections.original: Quapropter ?
sections.translation: For what reason ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.273b
sections.original: Quia ecastor mulier recte olet , ubi nihil olet .
sections.translation: Because , i ’ faith , a woman smells best [*] when she smells of nothing at all . For those old women who are in the habit of anointing themselves with unguents , vampt up creatures ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.275
sections.original: vetulae , edentulae , quae vitia corporis fuco occulunt ,
sections.translation: old hags , and toothless , who hide the blemishes of the person with paint , when the sweat has blended itself with the unguents , forthwith they stink just like when a cook has poured together a variety of broths ; what they smell of , you don ’ t know , except this only , that you understand that badly they do smell .

sections.label: Section 1.3.279
sections.original: Vt perdocte cuncta callet . nihil hac docta doctius .
sections.translation: How very cleverly she does understand everything ! There ’ s nothing more knowing than this knowing woman !

sections.label: Section 1.3.280
sections.original: verum illud ésse maxima adeo pars vestrorum intellegit ,
sections.translation: ( To the AUDIENCE .) This is the truth , and a very great portion , in fact , of you know it , who have old women for wives at home who purchased you with their portions .

sections.label: Section 1.3.282
sections.original: Agedum contempla aurum et pallam , satin haec me deceat , Scapha .
sections.translation: Come now , examine my golden trinkets and my mantle ; does this quite become me , Scapha ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.283
sections.original: Non me istuc curare oportet .
sections.translation: It befits not me to concern myself about that .

sections.label: Section 1.3.283b
sections.original: Quem obsecro igitur ?
sections.translation: Whom then , prithee ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.283c
sections.original: Eloquar :
sections.translation: I ’ ll tell you ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.284_285
sections.original: Philolachem , is ne quid emat , nisi quod sibi placere censeat .
sections.translation: Philolaches ; so that he may not buy anything except that which he fancies will please you . For a lover buys the favours of a mistress for himself with gold and purple garments . What need is there for that which he doesn ’ t want as his own , to be shown him still ? Age is to be enveloped in purple ; gold ornaments are unsuitable for a woman . A beautiful woman will be more beautiful naked than drest in purple .

sections.label: Section 1.3.290
sections.original: poste nequiquam exornata est bene , si morata est male .
sections.translation: Besides , it ’ s in vain she ’ s well-drest if she ’ s ill-conducted ; ill-conduct soils fine ornaments worse than dirt . But if she ’ s beauteous , she ’ s sufficiently adorned .

sections.label: Section 1.3.292b
sections.original: Nimis diu abstineo manum .
sections.translation: Too long have I withheld my hand . ( Coming forward .) What are you about here ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.293b
sections.original: Tibi me exorno út placeam .
sections.translation: I ’ m decking myself out to please you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.293c
sections.original: Ornata es satis .
sections.translation: You are dressed enough . ( To SCAPHA .) Go you hence in-doors , and take away this finery . ( SCAPHA goes into the house .) But , my delight ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.295
sections.original: mea Philematium , potare tecum conlibitum est mihi .
sections.translation: my Philematium , I have a mind to regale together with you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.296
sections.original: Et edepol mihi tecum , nam quod tibi libet idem mihi libet ,
sections.translation: And , i ’ faith , so I have with you ; for what you have a mind to , the same have I a mind to , my delight .

sections.label: Section 1.3.297b
sections.original: Em istuc verbum vile est viginti minis .
sections.translation: Ha ! at twenty mine that expression were cheap .

sections.label: Section 1.3.298
sections.original: Cedo , amabo , decem : bene emptum tibi dare hoc verbum volo .
sections.translation: Give me ten , there ’ s a dear ; I wish to let you have that expression bought a bargain .

sections.label: Section 1.3.299
sections.original: Etiam nunc decem minae apud te sunt ; vel rationem puta .
sections.translation: You ’ ve already got ten minae with you ; or reckon up the account :

sections.label: Section 1.3.300
sections.original: triginta minas pro capite tuo dedi .
sections.translation: thirty minae I gave for your freedom —

sections.label: Section 1.3.300b
sections.original: Cur exprobras ?
sections.translation: Why reproach me with that ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.301
sections.original: Egone id exprobrem , qui mihimet cupio id opprobrarier ?
sections.translation: What , I reproach you with it ? Why , I had rather that I myself were reproached with it ; no money whatever for this long time have I ever laid out equally well .

sections.label: Section 1.3.303
sections.original: Certe ego , quod te amo , operam nusquam melius potui ponere .
sections.translation: Surely , in loving you , I never could have better employed my pains .

sections.label: Section 1.3.304
sections.original: Bene igitur ratio accepti atque expensi inter nos convenit :
sections.translation: The account , then , of receipts and expenditure fully tallies between ourselves ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.305
sections.original: tu me amas , ego té amo ; merito id fieri uterque existimat .
sections.translation: you love me , I love you . Each thinks that it is so deservedly . Those who rejoice at this , may they ever rejoice at the continuance of their own happiness . Those who envy , let not any one henceforth be ever envious of their blessings .

sections.label: Section 1.3.308
sections.original: Age accumbe igitur . cedo aquam manibus , puere , appone hic mensulam .
sections.translation: Come , take your place , then . ( At the door , to a SERVANT , who obeys .) Boy , bring some water for the hands ; put a little table here . See where are the dice . Would you like some perfumes ? ( They recline on the couch .)

sections.label: Section 1.3.309b
sections.original: Quid opust ? cum stacta accubo .
sections.translation: What need is there ? Along with myrrh I am reclining .

sections.label: Section 1.3.310
sections.original: sed estne hic meus sodalis , qui huc incedit cúm amica sua ?
sections.translation: But isn ’ t this my friend who ’ s coming hither with his mistress ? ’ Tis he ; it ’ s Callidamates ; look , he ’ s coming . Capital ! my sweet one , see , our comrades are approaching ; they ’ re coming to share the spoil .

sections.label: Section 1.4.313
sections.original: Advérsum veníri mihi ád Philolachétem
sections.translation: I want you to come for me [*] in good time to the house of Philolaches ; listen you ; well then ! those are your orders . ( Exit SERVANT .)

sections.label: Section 1.4.315
sections.original: nam ílli ubi fui , inde éffugi foras ,
sections.translation: For from the place where I was , thence did I betake myself off ; so confoundedly tired was I there . with the entertainment and the discourse . Now I ’ ll go to Philolaches to have a bout there he ’ ll receive us with jovial feelings and handsomely . Do I seem to you to be fairly drenched , my bubsy ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.320
sections.original: Sémper istóc modo .
sections.translation: You ought always to live pursuing this course of life .

sections.label: Section 1.4.322
sections.original: Visne égo te ac tu me ampléctare ?
sections.translation: Should you like , then , for me to hug you , and you me ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.323
sections.original: Si tíbi cordi est faceré , licet .
sections.translation: If you ’ ve a mind to do so , of course .

sections.label: Section 1.4.323b
sections.original: Lépida es .
sections.translation: You are a charming one . ( He stumbles .) Do hold me up , there ’ s a dear .

sections.label: Section 1.4.324b
sections.original: Cave né cadas , ásta .
sections.translation: Take care you don ’ t fall . Stand up .

sections.label: Section 1.4.325
sections.original: O — o — ocellús meus , túos sum alumnus , mél meum .
sections.translation: O ! you are the apple of my eye . I ’ m your fosterling , my honey .

sections.label: Section 1.4.326
sections.original: Cáve modo , né prius ín via accúmbas ,
sections.translation: Only do take care that you don ’ t recline in the street , before we get to a place where a couch is ready laid .

sections.label: Section 1.4.328
sections.original: Síne , sine cádere me .
sections.translation: Do let me fall .

sections.label: Section 1.4.328b
sections.original: Síno , sed hoc , quod mi in manu est :
sections.translation: Well , I ’ ll let you . ( Lets go .)

sections.label: Section 1.4.330
sections.original: Iacéntis tollet póstea nos ámbos aliquis .
sections.translation: But that as well which I ’ ve got hold of in my hand .

sections.label: Section 1.4.331
sections.original: Madet hómo .
sections.translation: If you fall , you shan ’ t fall without me falling with you . Then some one shall pick us both up as we lie ( Aside .) The man ’ s quite drenched .

sections.label: Section 1.4.331b
sections.original: Tun me ais mammámadere ?
sections.translation: Do you say that I am drenched , my bubsy ?

sections.label: Section 1.4.332
sections.original: Cedo mánum , nolo equidem te ádfligi .
sections.translation: Give me your hand ; I really do not want ⋯ you hurt .

sections.label: Section 1.4.333
sections.original: Em téne .
sections.translation: There now , take it .

sections.label: Section 1.4.333b
sections.original: Age , i símul .
sections.translation: Come , move on with me .

sections.label: Section 1.4.333c
sections.original: Quo ego eam ?
sections.translation: Where am I going , do you know ?
